description: Persistence technique catalogue for AWS environments. Covers IAM, STS, EC2, Lambda, and storage persistence vectors. Used by scope-attack-paths Part 7 analysis.

iam.name: Create backdoor user
iam.what_attacker_achieves: New long-term credentials that survive rotation of the original
iam.notes: Survives credential rotation of the original compromised principal. Most durable persistence method.

iam.name: Backdoor role trust policy
iam.what_attacker_achieves: External attacker account can AssumeRole indefinitely
iam.notes: Modifies the trust policy of an existing role to add the attacker's account or principal. Survives incident response that only resets credentials.

iam.name: Backdoor policy version
iam.what_attacker_achieves: Hidden permissive policy version; attacker can switch default later
iam.notes: Creates a non-default policy version with broad permissions. The version is invisible unless auditors enumerate all policy versions. Attacker can flip it to default at any time.

iam.name: Add attacker MFA device
iam.what_attacker_achieves: Locks out legitimate user, attacker controls MFA
iam.notes: Attacker registers their own MFA device on the target user. If MFA is enforced, legitimate user loses access. Attacker retains it.

iam.name: Create/backdoor SAML/OIDC provider
iam.what_attacker_achieves: Federated access via attacker's identity provider
iam.notes: Attacker registers or modifies a SAML/OIDC provider pointing to infrastructure they control. Any role trusting that provider is now attacker-accessible.

iam.name: Disable MFA
iam.what_attacker_achieves: Removes MFA barrier for future access
iam.notes: Deactivates MFA on the compromised principal or other users, enabling password-only authentication for future sessions.

sts.name: Long-lived session tokens
sts.what_attacker_achieves: 36-hour tokens that survive key rotation and can't be enumerated
sts.notes: GetSessionToken issues tokens valid up to 36 hours. Unlike access keys, these tokens are not listed in IAM — defenders can't enumerate them. Tokens survive static credential rotation.

sts.name: Role chain juggling
sts.what_attacker_achieves: Infinite credential refresh loop — indefinite access with no long-term keys
sts.notes: Attacker assumes roles in a cycle (A assumes B, B assumes A or a third role). Each assumption refreshes credentials. With two mutually-trusting roles, access is indefinite with no static keys to rotate.

sts.name: Federation token console access
sts.what_attacker_achieves: Stealthy console access that doesn't appear in IAM user list
sts.notes: GetFederationToken creates long-lived (up to 36 hours) federated sessions. Sessions use the calling user's name as a federated identity — hard to distinguish from legitimate use.

ec2.name: Lifecycle Manager exfiltration
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Recurring AMI/snapshot sharing to attacker account
ec2.notes: DLM lifecycle policies can share snapshots/AMIs to external accounts on a schedule. Provides ongoing data exfiltration without repeated API calls.

ec2.name: Spot Fleet (long-lived)
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Up to 5-year compute with high-priv role, auto-beacons to attacker
ec2.notes: Spot Fleet requests can have validity periods up to 5 years. Instances run with the passed role and can beacon to attacker C2. Cheaper and longer-lived than On-Demand.

ec2.name: Backdoor launch template
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Every Auto Scaling instance runs attacker code / has attacker SSH key
ec2.notes: Modifies the default launch template version to include a malicious user data script or attacker SSH key. All future instances from that template are compromised at launch.

ec2.name: Replace root volume
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Swap root EBS to attacker-controlled volume; instance keeps its IPs and role
ec2.notes: Replaces the root EBS volume of a running instance with an attacker-controlled snapshot. The instance retains its IP addresses, security groups, and IAM role after the swap.

ec2.name: VPN into VPC
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Persistent network-level access into victim VPC
ec2.notes: Establishes a Site-to-Site VPN from attacker infrastructure into the victim VPC. Provides persistent L3 access that bypasses security groups and survives instance restarts.

ec2.name: VPC peering
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Direct routing between attacker and victim VPCs
ec2.notes: Creates a VPC peering connection between victim VPC and attacker-controlled VPC. Peering connections persist until explicitly deleted.

ec2.name: User data backdoor
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Malicious script runs on next instance start
ec2.notes: Modifies instance user data to include a backdoor script. The script executes on the next instance start/reboot, establishing persistence without modifying the AMI.

ec2.name: SSM State Manager
ec2.what_attacker_achieves: Recurring command execution on all SSM-managed instances (every 30 min+)
ec2.notes: SSM State Manager associations run documents on a schedule across all matching instances. Can target all instances in the account using wildcard resource targeting.

lambda.name: Lambda layer backdoor
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: Injected code runs on every invocation; function's own code appears clean
lambda.notes: Publishes a malicious Lambda layer and attaches it to target functions. The backdoor code in the layer executes before the function handler. The function's deployment package appears clean in code reviews.

lambda.name: Lambda extension
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: Separate process intercepts/modifies all requests; inherits execution role
lambda.notes: Lambda extensions run as a separate process alongside the function. Unlike layers, extensions can intercept requests/responses and run after the handler returns. They inherit the execution role.

lambda.name: Resource policy (cross-account invoke)
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: External account can invoke/update the function indefinitely
lambda.notes: Adds a resource-based policy granting an attacker-controlled AWS account permission to invoke the function. Survives role rotation and is easy to miss in IAM audits.

lambda.name: Weighted alias distribution
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: Backdoored version receives 1% of traffic — extremely stealthy
lambda.notes: Publishes a backdoored function version and creates/updates an alias with weighted routing (e.g., 99% legitimate, 1% backdoor). Very low traffic rate makes anomaly detection difficult.

lambda.name: EXEC_WRAPPER env var
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: Wrapper script executes before every handler; steals credentials
lambda.notes: Sets the AWS_LAMBDA_EXEC_WRAPPER environment variable to a path in an attacker-controlled layer. The wrapper runs before every handler invocation and can exfiltrate execution role credentials.

lambda.name: Async self-loop
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: Code-free heartbeat loop; function reinvokes itself via destinations
lambda.notes: Configures the function to invoke itself asynchronously via on-failure/on-success destinations. Creates a persistent execution loop that doesn't require external triggers.

lambda.name: Cron/Event trigger
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: Scheduled or event-driven execution of attacker function
lambda.notes: Creates an EventBridge rule targeting a Lambda function (attacker's or backdoored). Can trigger on a cron schedule or on specific AWS events (e.g., console login, role assumption).

lambda.name: Alias-scoped resource policy
lambda.what_attacker_achieves: Hidden invoke permission on specific backdoored version only
lambda.notes: Adds a resource policy with a --qualifier scoped to a specific alias or version. The permission only appears when enumerating that specific qualifier, making it easy to miss in broad audits.

lambda.name: Freeze runtime version
lambda.notes: Pins the Lambda runtime to a specific version using manual update mode. Prevents AWS from auto-applying runtime patches. Preserves any runtime-level vulnerabilities the attacker is exploiting.

storage.name: S3 ACL backdoor
storage.what_attacker_achieves: Full control via ACLs — often overlooked in audits
storage.notes: Grants an attacker-controlled account full control via S3 bucket ACLs. ACLs are often overlooked when auditing bucket policies. Persists until explicitly removed.

storage.name: KMS key policy backdoor
storage.what_attacker_achieves: External account gets permanent decrypt access to all data using that key
storage.notes: Modifies the KMS key policy to grant the attacker's account kms:Decrypt and kms:GenerateDataKey. All data encrypted with that key is permanently accessible to the attacker.

storage.name: KMS eternal grant
storage.what_attacker_achieves: Self-renewing grants — attacker can re-create grants even if some are revoked
storage.notes: Creates a KMS grant with CreateGrant permission in the grant constraints. Even if defenders revoke the grant, the attacker can recreate it. Grants bypass IAM policy and persist independently.

storage.name: Secrets Manager resource policy
storage.notes: Attaches a resource policy to a Secrets Manager secret granting the attacker's account GetSecretValue. Survives secret rotation — the policy persists on the secret regardless of value changes.

storage.name: Malicious rotation Lambda
storage.what_attacker_achieves: Every scheduled rotation exfiltrates current secret values
storage.notes: Points the secret's rotation Lambda to an attacker-controlled function. Every subsequent rotation event calls the malicious function, which exfiltrates the new secret value before completing rotation.

storage.name: Version stage hijacking
storage.what_attacker_achieves: Hidden secret version; attacker atomically flips AWSCURRENT on demand
storage.notes: Creates a hidden secret version with a known value. The attacker can atomically promote it to AWSCURRENT at a chosen moment, poisoning all systems that read the secret.

storage.name: Cross-region replica promotion
storage.what_attacker_achieves: Standalone replica under attacker KMS key in untrusted region
storage.notes: Replicates the secret to a region encrypted with an attacker-controlled KMS key, then stops replication to make the replica standalone. The replica retains the current secret value and is independently accessible.
